title: ML Research Papers
description: Ten canonical ML papers every FAANG candidate must know — from Attention and ResNets to GANs, VAEs, diffusion models, and scaling laws — with the key formula, intuition, and interview angle for each.
details: This file covers 10 foundational ML papers with FAANG-depth analysis: Transformer (Attention Is All You Need), ResNet (Deep Residual Learning), Batch Normalization, Adam, Dropout, GANs, VAEs, BERT, Scaling Laws, and DDPMs. Each entry includes a concise summary, the key formula, [Intuition] intuition, and [Interview Angle] FAANG interview angle. Every paper was published at a top venue (NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, ACL, CVPR) and has shaped modern deep learning. Together they represent the technical knowledge expected at the L4–L5 level across Google, Meta, OpenAI, and Amazon.

sections.title: Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al. 2017)
sections.description:
Summary: Introduced the Transformer architecture — replacing RNN recurrence with multi-head self-attention. Key innovations: scaled dot-product attention (QKᵀ/√d), multi-head attention (h heads, d/h each), positional encoding (sin/cos), and the encoder-decoder architecture.

[Intuition] Intuition: The scaling factor 1/√d keeps the variance of the dot product ≈ 1, so softmax doesn't saturate. Without scaling, for large d the dot products have variance d, causing softmax to concentrate on a single token (near-one-hot), giving vanishing gradients.

[Interview Angle] FAANG: "Why scale by 1/√d?" Because Qᵢ·Kⱼ = Σₗ Qᵢₗ·Kⱼₗ where each term has variance 1 (unit variance initialization), so the sum has variance d. Dividing by √d normalizes variance back to ~1, keeping softmax in the linear regime. Also: "Why multi-head?" Different heads learn different attention patterns (syntactic, semantic, positional).

[Practical Workflow] How to discuss in an interview: Start with the core insight: parallelizable attention replaces recurrence. Then mention scaled dot-product and why √d matters. Close with the impact: Transformers enabled GPT, BERT, and the entire LLM ecosystem. Interviewers want to hear you understand why attention works, not just what it does.

[Paper] Attention Is All You Need — arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762 (NeurIPS 2017)

sections.title: Deep Residual Learning (He et al. 2016)
sections.description:
Summary: Skip connections (identity shortcut) allow training 100+ layer networks. ResNet-152 achieves 4.5% top-5 error on ImageNet vs VGG's 7.3% with fewer FLOPs. The core idea: learn residual F(x) = H(x) - x rather than the full mapping H(x).

[Intuition] Intuition: If adding more layers doesn't help, the residual connection lets them learn identity — the network can choose to ignore extra layers. The gradient flows through the identity path: ∂L/∂x = ∂L/∂y · (∂F/∂x + 1). The +1 is a gradient highway — even if ∂F/∂x → 0, gradients still flow.

[Interview Angle] FAANG: "Why do residual connections help gradient flow?" Because the identity path gives the gradient a direct route to early layers, bypassing the non-linearities. "What's the difference between ResNet and DenseNet?" ResNet sums F(x) + x; DenseNet concatenates [F(x), x], growing channel dimension.

[Practical Workflow] How to discuss in an interview: Frame ResNet as solving the degradation problem (deeper nets perform worse, not due to overfitting). The identity shortcut creates a gradient highway — this is the key interview point. Mention that residual connections are now universal (Transformers, Diffusion models, ResNets).

[Paper] Deep Residual Learning — arxiv.org/abs/1512.03385 (CVPR 2016)

sections.title: Batch Normalization (Ioffe & Szegedy 2015)
sections.description:
Summary: Normalize each layer's pre-activations: ẑ = (z - μ_B)/σ_B, then scale+shift: y = γẑ + β. Learnable γ,β restore the layer's representational power. At inference, use running μ,σ (population statistics) instead of batch stats.

[Intuition] Intuition: The original paper credited "reducing internal covariate shift" — but later work (Santurkar et al. 2018) showed it's actually the smoothing of the loss landscape that helps. BatchNorm makes gradients more predictive, enabling higher learning rates.

Key formula:
    μ_B = (1/m) Σ xᵢ    — batch mean
    σ_B² = (1/m) Σ (xᵢ - μ_B)²  — batch variance
    ẑᵢ = (xᵢ - μ_B) / √(σ_B² + ε)  — normalize
    yᵢ = γ · ẑᵢ + β        — scale + shift (learnable)

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Inception-v3 with BN trains 14× faster (fewer epochs) and allows 5× higher learning rate. BN adds ~2 additional parameters per channel (γ, β) — negligible overhead.

[Interview Angle] FAANG: "Why does BatchNorm need different behavior at train vs inference?" At train time, μ_B,σ_B are from the batch — adds noise that regularizes. At inference, using batch stats would make predictions batch-dependent (non-deterministic). So we use running averages computed during training. "Why can't we use BN with batch size 1?" σ_B would be 0 — division by zero. Use LayerNorm instead.

[Practical Workflow] How to discuss in an interview: The key insight is that BN smooths the loss landscape (not just reducing internal covariate shift). Show depth by explaining the train/test discrepancy: batch stats vs running averages. Mention that modern LLMs prefer LayerNorm because BN breaks at small batch sizes and with variable-length sequences.

[Paper] Batch Normalization — arxiv.org/abs/1502.03167 (ICML 2015)

sections.title: Adam Optimizer (Kingma & Ba 2015)
sections.description:
Summary: Adaptive Moment Estimation combines momentum (first moment) with per-parameter learning rates (second moment). Each parameter has its own adaptive LR based on the magnitude of its gradients.

Let g_t = gradient at step t
  m_t = β₁·m_{t-1} + (1-β₁)·g_t        ← running mean (1st moment)
  v_t = β₂·v_{t-1} + (1-β₂)·g_t²       ← running variance (2nd moment, uncentered)
  m̂_t = m_t / (1 - β₁ᵗ)                  ← bias correction
  v̂_t = v_t / (1 - β₂ᵗ)
  θ_{t+1} = θ_t - α · m̂_t / (√v̂_t + ε)  ← normalized step

Defaults: β₁=0.9, β₂=0.999, ε=1e⁻⁸, α=1e⁻³ (default LR for transformers)

[Intuition] Intuition: Parameters with consistent gradient direction get larger steps (momentum), and parameters with high variance gradients get smaller steps (normalization by √v).

[Interview Angle] FAANG: "Why does Adam need bias correction?" At early steps, m_t and v_t are initialized at 0, so they're biased toward 0. Without correction, early steps would be tiny. Bias correction amplifies them: m̂₁ = g₁ / (1 - 0.9) = 10·g₁, fixing the initialization bias. "What's the difference between Adam and AdamW?" AdamW decouples weight decay from the gradient update: θ += -α·m̂/(√v̂+ε) - α·λ·θ. In Adam, weight decay = L2 regularization (adds λ·θ to g), which interacts badly with the adaptive LR.

[Practical Workflow] How to discuss in an interview: Know the full Adam update formula from memory — interviewers often ask you to write it out. The two key innovations: momentum (first moment) and per-parameter LR (second moment). Mention bias correction and why it's needed. Distinguish Adam from AdamW (decoupled weight decay) — this is the preferred optimizer for LLMs.

[Paper] Adam Optimizer — arxiv.org/abs/1412.6980 (ICLR 2015)

sections.title: Dropout (Srivastava et al. 2014)
sections.description:
Summary: Randomly drop neurons with probability p during training. At test time, use all neurons scaled by p. Inverted dropout (standard today): scale activations by 1/(1-p) during training, no scaling at test time. Prevents co-adaptation — acts as an ensemble of 2^n sub-networks.

[Intuition] Intuition: By randomly removing neurons, you force the network to learn redundant representations — no single neuron can be critical. Each neuron must be useful in combination with many different subsets of other neurons.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete: Dropout p=0.5 on AlexNet reduces test error from 17.0% to 15.6% (top-5 on ImageNet). Optimal p: 0.5 for hidden layers (maximum regularization), 0.2–0.3 for input (don't drop too many features).

[Interview Angle] FAANG: "What's inverted dropout?" During training, multiply by 1/(1-p) to keep expected activation constant; at test time, no scaling. This means inference code is identical whether dropout was used or not. "Why not use dropout in transformers?" Transformers use LayerNorm + residual connections that conflict with dropout; instead, use attention dropout (drop attention weights) and stochastic depth (drop entire layers).

[Practical Workflow] How to discuss in an interview: Frame dropout as an ensemble method — training 2ⁿ sub-networks with weight sharing. Explain inverted dropout (scale at train time, not test). Mention that in the LLM era, dropout is used less — LayerNorm and residual connections provide regularization — but it remains essential for smaller models (<100M params).

[Paper] Dropout — JMLR 2014

sections.title: Generative Adversarial Networks (Goodfellow et al. 2014)
sections.description:
Summary: Two networks in a min-max game: Generator G(z) maps noise to fake data; Discriminator D(x) distinguishes real from fake. Nash equilibrium at D(x) = 0.5 for all x (D can't distinguish real from fake).

[Intuition] Intuition: Think of G as a forger and D as a detective. They get better together: G learns to create more convincing fakes, D learns to spot them. The competition drives both to improve — G learns the data distribution without ever seeing real data directly.

[Interview Angle] FAANG: "Why does GAN training oscillate? Because D and G compete in a two-player game — no single objective converges. The loss doesn't act as a reliable metric. Solutions: label smoothing, gradient penalty (WGAN-GP), spectral normalization. "What's mode collapse?" G learns to produce only a few modes of the data distribution (e.g., only one digit in MNIST). The generator finds a "cheap" way to fool D without covering the full distribution.

[Practical Workflow] How to discuss in an interview: The min-max game is the fundamental contribution. Know the Nash equilibrium: D(x)=0.5. Discuss training instability (mode collapse, non-convergence) and solutions (WGAN, LSGAN, spectral norm). GANs are largely replaced by diffusion models for image generation, but the adversarial concept remains influential.

[Paper] Generative Adversarial Networks — arxiv.org/abs/1406.2661 (NeurIPS 2014)

sections.title: Variational Autoencoders (Kingma & Welling 2014)
sections.description:
Summary: A generative model with an encoder q_φ(z|x) that maps data to a latent distribution and decoder p_θ(x|z) that reconstructs data. Key formula: ELBO = E[log p(x|z)] - D_KL(q(z|x)||p(z)). The reparameterization trick (z = μ + σ⊙ε, ε∼N(0,I)) allows gradient flow through the sampling operation.

[Intuition] Intuition: The reparameterization trick moves the randomness from the network parameters to the input noise — gradients can now flow through the sampling operation. Without this trick, we couldn't backprop through a random node. The KL divergence term acts as a regularizer, keeping the latent distribution close to N(0,I) so the decoder can generate from any point in the latent space.

Key difference from autoencoder:
  AE:  z = encoder(x);  x̂ = decoder(z);  loss = ||x - x̂||²  (no constraints on z)
  VAE: z = μ + σ·ε;     x̂ = decoder(z);  loss = ||x - x̂||² + D_KL(N(μ,σ²) || N(0,1))
       
The KL term pushes μ → 0, σ → 1, making the latent space continuous and complete.

[Interview Angle] FAANG: "Explain the reparameterization trick." Instead of sampling z ∼ N(μ, σ²) (non-differentiable), sample ε ∼ N(0,1) and compute z = μ + σ·ε. This makes z differentiable w.r.t. μ and σ because the randomness ε is independent of the network parameters. "What's the ELBO trade-off?" Reconstruction vs. regularization: β-VAE uses β > 1 to encourage more disentangled representations, at the cost of reconstruction quality.

[Practical Workflow] How to discuss in an interview: The reparameterization trick is the key technical insight — explain it clearly: move randomness from params to input noise. The ELBO trade-off (reconstruction vs KL) is the conceptual foundation. Distinguish VAEs from GANs (VAEs optimize a bound, GANs play a game) and diffusion models (VAEs use a single latent, diffusion uses a chain).

[Paper] Variational Autoencoders — arxiv.org/abs/1312.6114 (ICLR 2014)

sections.title: BERT (Devlin et al. 2019)
sections.description:
Summary: Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Pre-trained on BooksCorpus + English Wikipedia (3.3B words) using two objectives: (1) Masked Language Model (MLM) — 15% of tokens masked, predict them; (2) Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) — predict if sentence B follows sentence A. Fine-tune on downstream tasks.

[Intuition] Intuition: Unlike GPT (left-to-right), BERT sees the full context around each token. Masking forces the model to use both left and right context to predict the missing word — this is the key difference from autoregressive LMs. The [CLS] token's final hidden state serves as the aggregate sequence representation for classification tasks.

BERT-base: 12 layers, 768 hidden, 12 heads, 110M params
BERT-large: 24 layers, 1024 hidden, 16 heads, 340M params
Pre-training: 4M steps on 16 TPU chips (4 days), batch size 256, max seq len 512
Learning rate: 1e⁻⁴ with 10K warmup steps and linear decay

[Interview Angle] FAANG: "Why does BERT use MLM instead of autoregressive LM?" Bidirectional context captures richer representations. Unlike GPT, the model sees both left and right context, which is critical for tasks requiring full context (QA, NER, classification). "What's the problem with [MASK] tokens?" The [MASK] token never appears in fine-tuning, creating a mismatch. Solution: don't always replace with [MASK] — 80% [MASK], 10% random token, 10% unchanged. "BERT vs GPT?" BERT is encoder-only (bidirectional), GPT is decoder-only (autoregressive). BERT excels at understanding tasks, GPT at generation.

[Practical Workflow] How to discuss in an interview: BERT introduced the pre-train + fine-tune paradigm that dominates NLP. The MLM objective is the key differentiator from GPT — bidirectional context matters for understanding tasks. Discuss the [MASK] mismatch problem and the 80/10/10 replacement strategy. BERT's architecture (encoder-only) is now standard for embeddings and retrieval.

[Paper] BERT — arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805 (NAACL 2019)

sections.title: Scaling Laws (Kaplan et al. 2020)
sections.description:
Summary: Power-law relationships between model size (N), data (D), compute (C), and loss (L). Key result: L ∝ N^(-0.076) for parameters, L ∝ D^(-0.095) for data, L ∝ C^(-0.050) for compute. Optimal allocation: ~8.3× more tokens than parameters (Kaplan) or ~20× (Chinchilla).

[Intuition] Intuition: Double the model size → halve the loss. But you also need ~8× more data for that bigger model to be useful. The bitter lesson: general methods that scale with compute consistently beat hand-engineered approaches in the long run.

[Concrete Numbers] Concrete numbers:

  • GPT-3 175B: 300B tokens, 3.14E23 FLOPs training compute
  • LLaMA 65B: 1.4T tokens (Chinchilla-optimal)
  • GPT-4 (estimated): 1.8T params (MoE), ~2E25 FLOPs
  • Training a 175B model costs: 3.14E23 FLOPs ÷ 312 TFLOPS (A100) = ~10⁶ GPU-hours ≈ $4M

[Interview Angle] FAANG: "How many FLOPs to train a 175B model?" ≈ 3.14E23 FLOPs = 6·N·D = 6 × 175B × 300B. Memo: FLOPs ≈ 6·N·D for standard autoregressive training. "What's Chinchilla's insight?" Most LLMs were undertrained — for a given compute budget, use more tokens and fewer parameters. The optimal ratio is ~20 tokens per parameter, not the ~1.7 used by GPT-3.

[Practical Workflow] How to discuss in an interview: The key insight: performance follows power laws in N, D, and C. Know the numbers: L ∝ N^(-0.076), L ∝ D^(-0.095). The Chinchilla finding (20 tokens/param) means most pre-2022 LLMs were undertrained. Use scaling laws to justify investment in data quality over model size — this shows strategic thinking.

[Paper] Scaling Laws — arxiv.org/abs/2001.08361 (NeurIPS 2020 workshop)

sections.title: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (Ho et al. 2020)
sections.description:
Summary: Forward process gradually adds Gaussian noise over T steps (typically 1000). Reverse process learns to denoise step by step. Key formula: q(x_t|x_{t-1}) = N(x_t; √(1-β_t)·x_{t-1}, β_tI). The model predicts noise ε_θ(x_t, t), and the reverse step computes x_{t-1} from x_t.

[Intuition] Intuition: Corrupt an image with noise over 1000 steps until it becomes pure Gaussian. Then learn to reverse this process step by step. At each reverse step, the model predicts the noise added at that step and removes it. With enough steps, the denoising distribution is approximately Gaussian (small step assumption), making each reverse step a simple Gaussian prediction.

DDPM objective: L = E_{t,x₀,ε}[||ε - ε_θ(√ᾱₜ·x₀ + √(1-ᾱₜ)·ε, t)||²]
    where ᾱₜ = Πₛ₌₁ᵗ (1 - βₛ), β_t is the noise schedule

DDIM: Deterministic reverse process for faster sampling (10-50 steps vs 1000)
CFG:  ε̂ = ε_θ(x_t) + w·(ε_θ(x_t,c) - ε_θ(x_t))  — classifier-free guidance
    w > 1 trades diversity for fidelity (w=7.5 is standard for Stable Diffusion)

[Interview Angle] FAANG: "How does classifier-free guidance work?" CFG interpolates between conditional and unconditional noise predictions: ε̂ = ε_u + w·(ε_c - ε_u). The unconditional model ε_u = ε_θ(x_t, ∅) uses a null token as condition. w > 1 pushes the sampling toward conditional modes (better alignment with prompt but less diversity). "DDPM vs DDIM?" DDPM is stochastic (adds noise in reverse), DDIM is deterministic (no noise added) — DDIM can use fewer steps using the same pretrained weights. "Why is DDPM slow?" 1000 sequential denoising steps. Distillation (Progressive Distillation, Consistency Models) reduces to 1-4 steps.

[Practical Workflow] How to discuss in an interview: Frame diffusion as the successor to GANs — stable training, mode coverage, but slower sampling. The key formula: ELBO over timesteps = Σₜ D_KL(q(xₜ₋₁|xₜ,x₀) || p_θ(xₜ₋₁|xₜ)). Know the noise schedule (linear β_t from 10⁻⁴ to 0.02 over T=1000 steps) and the reparameterization: x_t = √ᾱ·x₀ + √(1-ᾱ)·ε.

[Paper] Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models — arxiv.org/abs/2006.11239 (NeurIPS 2020)

sections.title: FAANG Interview Q&A
sections.description:
[Interview Angle] Q1: "What's the key insight in the Transformer paper and why does it matter?" — Google L4

The key insight is that attention alone is sufficient to model sequential data — you don't need recurrence or convolution. The Transformer uses scaled dot-product attention over the full sequence in parallel (O(T²·d) vs RNN's O(T·d²) but fully parallelizable). This enabled training on much larger datasets because attention is compute-bound (high parallelism) while RNNs are memory-bound (sequential). The scaling of attention to 100K+ tokens (via FlashAttention, RingAttention) is why models like GPT-4 can handle long contexts. The multi-head design (h=8–96 heads) lets each head specialize in different relationships (syntax, semantics, position).

[Interview Angle] Q2: "Explain the reparameterization trick in VAEs." — Meta L5

The reparameterization trick makes sampling differentiable. Instead of sampling z ∼ N(μ, σ²) (a stochastic node with no gradient), we sample ε ∼ N(0, 1) and compute z = μ + σ·ε. The gradient flows through μ and σ because ε is an input, not a sampling operation. Without this trick, we can't backprop through the encoder to minimize the KL divergence. This is the key innovation that made VAEs trainable — earlier attempts (Helmholtz machines, wake-sleep) used less efficient gradient estimation. The trade-off: VAE samples are often blurry because the KL term pulls the posterior toward a simple Gaussian prior, smoothing out fine details. This motivated more expressive priors (NVP, IAF, diffusion-based priors).

[Interview Angle] Q3: "What do scaling laws tell us about training larger models?" — OpenAI L5, Google L5

Scaling laws (Kaplan et al. 2020, Hoffmann et al. 2022) show three key results: (1) Performance follows a power law with model size, data, and compute — increasing any resource improves performance predictably until another resource is the bottleneck. (2) The optimal allocation for a given compute budget is ~20 tokens per parameter (Chinchilla), not the ~1.7 used by GPT-3. This means most large models were undertrained relative to their parameter count. (3) FLOPs ≈ 6·N·D for training (forward + backward, 2 FLOPs per param per token per direction × 3 steps). Implications: scaling laws predict that training a 10× larger model requires 10× more data and 10× more compute for proportional gains. This is why the industry shifted to massive data collection and why data quality (dedup, filtering) now matters more than sheer quantity. The existence of predictable scaling means you can extrapolate performance before training — a huge advantage for resource planning.

[Interview Angle] Q4: "Compare ResNet and Transformer — what design pattern do they share?" — Amazon L5

Both use residual connections as the critical architectural pattern. ResNet's y = F(x) + x allows training 152-layer networks; Transformer's x = x + Attention(LN(x)) enables 96-layer GPT models. Both also use normalization (BatchNorm in ResNet, LayerNorm in Transformer). The residual connection is the single most impactful design pattern in modern deep learning — it appears in every architecture: ResNet, Transformer, UNet (diffusion), GAN discriminator, etc. The key difference: ResNet's residual blocks are convolutional (spatial feature extraction), while Transformer's are attention-based (pairwise token relationships). The similarity: both stack identical blocks with residual connections, enabling depth through the gradient highway (the +1 identity term).

[Interview Angle] Q5: "How does classifier-free guidance work in diffusion models?" — OpenAI L5

CFG modifies the noise prediction: ε̂ = ε_θ(x_t, ∅) + w · (ε_θ(x_t, c) - ε_θ(x_t, ∅)). The unconditional prediction ε_θ(x_t, ∅) is obtained by conditioning on a null/corrupted text embedding. The guidance scale w controls the trade-off: w=1 gives standard conditional sampling, w>1 amplifies the conditioning signal (better alignment, less diversity). w=7.5 is standard in Stable Diffusion. CFG works because diffusion models are trained with both conditional and unconditional objectives (10% of training steps use the null condition), so the model learns both ε_θ(x_t, c) and ε_θ(x_t, ∅). Without CFG, diffusion samples are often less coherent because the conditional signal is weak for individual denoising steps.

  • Batch Normalization — Ioffe & Szegedy 2015: arxiv.org/abs/1502.03167 (ICML 2015)
  • Adam — Kingma & Ba 2015: arxiv.org/abs/1412.6980 (ICLR 2015)
